w:r.w:t: EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON THE SOCIETY
Based on "Climate Change: Its Causes and Effects"
Premium Study Resource for Students • Aligned with Curriculum Objectives
Climate Change: Its Causes and Effects
Adapted from United Nations climate change resources
Understanding climate change, its causes, impacts, and solutions
Reading comprehension, critical thinking, cause-effect analysis, fact vs. opinion differentiation
Discuss the picture and predict the theme of the text
What do you already know about climate change?
Connects personal experience to academic content
How might climate change affect your community?
: Long-term alterations in Earth's average weather patterns (temperature, precipitation, wind, etc.)
: Both natural processes AND human activities
: Increased greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, and industrial processes
: Affects health, food security, housing, safety, and work
: People in small island nations and developing countries face sea-level rise, saltwater intrusion, drought, and famine risks
: Weather-related displacement expected to rise significantly
Part 2: Major Causes of Climate Change
Coal, oil, natural gas release CO₂ and greenhouse gases for electricity, heat, transportation
Factories emit methane (CH₄), nitrous oxide (N₂O); manufacturing uses fossil fuels for energy
Cutting forests reduces CO₂ absorption; cut trees release stored carbon
~12 million hectares destroyed yearly; ~25% of global emissions
Cars, trucks, ships, planes run on fossil fuels; road vehicles largest contributor
Emissions from deforestation for agriculture, livestock digestion, fertilizers, farm equipment, packaging
Major contributor via CO₂, methane, other gases
Landfills release methane from organic waste decomposition; incineration releases CO₂ and toxins
Methane is 25x more potent than CO₂ at trapping heat
Rising energy demand for buildings, heating/cooling, appliances; increased consumption
Buildings consume over half of all electricity globally
Part 3: Major Effects of Climate Change
Warmer temperatures → more moisture → extreme rainfall/flooding; warm oceans fuel tropical storms
Destructive storms damage homes, cause deaths, economic losses
Climate change alters water availability; exacerbates shortages in water-stressed regions
Agricultural/ecological droughts; dust storms; desert expansion; food/water insecurity
Ocean absorbs most heat from global warming; water expands when warm; melting ice sheets raise sea levels
Threatens coastal/island communities; ocean acidification endangers marine life
Climate risks increase as temperatures climb; species loss rate 1,000x greater than historical average
1 million species at risk of extinction; forests fires, invasive species, extreme weather as threats
Climate changes and extreme weather destroy/reduce productivity of fisheries, crops, livestock
Ocean acidification threatens marine food sources; heat stress reduces grazing lands
Climate change is "the single biggest health threat facing humanity"
Air pollution, disease spread, extreme weather deaths, mental health pressures, malnutrition; ~13 million deaths/year from environmental factors
Part 4: The Way Forward – Solutions at All Levels
Adopt sustainable lifestyles: conserve energy, reduce waste, use public transport, support renewable energy, make climate-conscious diet/consumption choices
Promote environmental awareness; implement local adaptation strategies; invest in green infrastructure; support conservation and resilience-building projects
Enforce international agreements (e.g., Paris Agreement); transition to clean energy economies; strengthen climate policies; provide financial/technological support to developing countries
Threatens: environment, biodiversity, human health, food security, socio-economic stability
Only collective responsibility and sustained action
can slow climate change and secure a sustainable planet

"Climate change refers to... primarily caused by natural processes and human activities, such as the increased emission of greenhouse gases from fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, and industrial processes."
"These effects are interconnected and worsening, affecting both natural systems and human communities, particularly the most vulnerable populations."
"Some of us are already more vulnerable to climate impacts, such as people living in small island nations and other developing countries."
"Addressing climate change requires urgent and coordinated action at all levels."
"Only through collective responsibility and sustained action can we slow down climate change and secure a sustainable and liveable planet for future generations."
"Climate change refers to long-term alterations in the average weather patterns..."
Establishes clear understanding of key term
"Major Causes: 1. Burning of Fossil Fuels; 2. Industrial Activities; 3. Deforestation..."
"As temperatures rise, more moisture evaporates, which exacerbates extreme rainfall and flooding"
Helps readers understand relationships between phenomena
"Approximately 12 million hectares of forest are destroyed"; "Methane is over 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide"
Adds credibility and specificity to arguments
"Some of us are already more vulnerable"; "Only through collective responsibility"
"Individually, people must adopt sustainable lifestyles... Globally, governments must commit..."
Q1: What is climate change and what are its main causes?

Climate change refers to long-term alterations in Earth's average weather patterns and conditions, including temperature, precipitation, wind, and other climate variables. Its main causes include both natural processes and human activities, primarily: (1) burning fossil fuels

(coal, oil, natural gas) for electricity, heat, and transportation; (2) industrial activities emitting methane and nitrous oxide; (3) deforestation reducing CO₂ absorption; (4) transportation emissions; (5) food production emissions; (6) waste management releasing methane; and (7) overconsumption and urbanization increasing energy demand.
Q2: How does climate change affect water availability in different regions?

Climate change is changing water availability, making it scarcer in more regions. Global warming exacerbates water shortages in already water-stressed regions and increases the risk of agricultural droughts (affecting crops) and ecological droughts (increasing ecosystem vulnerability). Droughts can also trigger destructive sand and dust storms, expand deserts, and reduce land available for growing food, putting many people at risk of not having enough water regularly.
Q3: How does climate change impact biodiversity and species survival?

Climate change poses risks to the survival of species on land and in the ocean, with risks increasing as temperatures climb. The world is losing species at a rate 1,000 times greater than at any other time in recorded human history, with one million species at risk of extinction within the next few decades. Threats include forest fires, extreme weather, and invasive pests and diseases. While some species may relocate and survive, others will not.
Q4: How is the increase in poverty an effect of climate change?

Climate change contributes to poverty through multiple interconnected pathways: (1) Food scarcity from destroyed or less productive fisheries, crops, and livestock; (2) Displacement from sea-level rise, storms, and droughts forcing communities to relocate; (3) Health risks increasing medical costs and reducing workforce productivity; (4) Economic losses from destructive storms and extreme weather events. These effects disproportionately impact vulnerable populations, deepening existing inequalities.
Q5: How does deforestation contribute to climate change?

Deforestation contributes to climate change in two key ways: (1) Cutting down forests reduces the number of trees available to absorb CO₂ from the atmosphere; (2) When trees are cut, they release the carbon they have been storing. Deforestation, together with agriculture and other land use changes, is responsible for roughly a quarter of global greenhouse gas emissions.
Q6: Why might people in small island nations be more affected by climate change than others?

People in small island nations are more vulnerable because: (1) Sea-level rise and saltwater intrusion have advanced to the point where whole communities have had to relocate; (2) These nations often have limited resources for adaptation and mitigation; (3) They face disproportionate impacts from extreme weather events like cyclones and hurricanes that feed on warm ocean waters; (4) Their economies often depend on climate-sensitive sectors like fishing and tourism.
Q7: Why is the ocean both a victim and a helper in the fight against climate change?
because it soaks up most of the heat from global warming and absorbs carbon dioxide, keeping it from the atmosphere. However, it is a

because: (1) As it warms, its volume increases (water expands when warm), contributing to sea-level rise; (2) Absorbing more CO₂ makes the ocean more acidic, endangering marine life and coral reefs; (3) Melting ice sheets caused by warming also raise sea levels, threatening coastal communities.
Q8: Do you think switching to renewable energy can solve all climate problems? Why or why not?

While switching to renewable energy is crucial, the text suggests it cannot solve all climate problems alone. Climate change has multiple interconnected causes (deforestation, waste management, food production, etc.) and effects (biodiversity loss, health risks, displacement). The text emphasizes that solutions require "urgent and coordinated action at all levels"—individual, community, and global—including sustainable lifestyles, green infrastructure, international agreements, AND financial/technological support to developing countries. Renewable energy is essential but must be part of a comprehensive strategy.
Q9: What connection can you draw between climate change and poverty?

The text shows climate change and poverty are mutually reinforcing: (1) Climate impacts (droughts, storms, sea-level rise) destroy livelihoods, food sources, and homes, pushing people into poverty; (2) Poor communities often lack resources to adapt or recover from climate disasters; (3) Climate change exacerbates food insecurity and health risks, which disproportionately affect the poor; (4) The text notes that "protracted droughts are putting people at risk of famine" and that vulnerable populations in developing countries face the greatest risks.
Q10: Why is climate change considered a global issue, not just a local or national one?

Climate change is global because: (1) Greenhouse gases emitted anywhere affect the entire planet's atmosphere; (2) Effects like rising sea levels, species extinction, and extreme weather cross national borders; (3) Solutions require international cooperation (e.g., Paris Agreement); (4) The text states that addressing climate change needs "coordinated action at all levels" and that governments must "provide financial and technological support to developing countries." No single nation can solve it alone.
1. Burning fossil fuels releases greenhouse gases into the atmosphere.
"Coal, oil, and natural gas release large amounts of carbon dioxide (CO₂) and other greenhouse gases"
2. Deforestation helps reduce carbon dioxide in the air.
"Cutting down forests reduces the number of trees that absorb CO₂, increasing the concentration of greenhouse gases"
3. Methane is a greenhouse gas that is more powerful than carbon dioxide.
"Methane is over 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide (CO₂) in trapping heat"
4. Industrial activities have no role in contributing to global warming.
"Factories and industries emit various greenhouse gases... contributing to global warming"
5. Transportation, especially road vehicles, is a major source of carbon dioxide emissions.
"Road vehicles account for the largest part... Transport accounts for nearly one quarter of global energy-related carbon-dioxide emissions"
6. The ocean absorbs both heat and carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
"The ocean soaks up most of the heat from global warming... the ocean absorbs carbon dioxide, keeping it from the atmosphere"
7. Climate change has no impact on human health.
"Climate change is the single biggest health threat facing humanity"
8. More acidic oceans can harm marine life and coral reefs.
"More carbon dioxide makes the ocean more acidic, which endangers marine life and coral reefs"
9. Climate change only affects animals, not humans.
Text details impacts on human health, food security, housing, safety, work, and displacement
10. Switching to sustainable practices can help reduce the effects of climate change.
"Only through collective responsibility and sustained action can we slow down climate change"
1. What is the primary cause of the recent changes in Earth's climate patterns?
B) Human activities such as fossil fuel use and deforestation
C) Changes in the Earth's orbit
2. Which greenhouse gas is over 25 times more potent than CO₂ in trapping heat?
3. Which sector is identified as one of the largest contributors to global greenhouse gas emissions?
4. What major environmental issue results from deforestation?
D) Lower oxygen levels in oceans
5. Why might coastal communities be at greater risk from climate change?
A) They are generally more developed
B) They lack access to renewable energy
C) Rising sea levels and storm surges threaten their existence
D) They depend heavily on air travel
6. What would be a logical consequence if sustainable waste practices are not adopted?
A) Decrease in greenhouse gas emissions
C) Increased methane emissions and environmental degradation
7. What is the main purpose of the passage?
C) To inform and persuade action on climate change
D) To promote new technology for farming
8. How does climate change impact water availability?
(Text: "Climate change is changing water availability, making it scarcer in more regions... increased risk of agricultural droughts")
9. What happens as ocean temperatures rise?
(Text: "As the ocean warms, its volume increases since water expands as it gets warmer")
10. What is a major economic impact of climate change?
B) Release of gases/substances into the atmosphere
14. What does "mitigation" mean in climate context?
B) Actions to reduce severity or impact of climate change
15. What does "adaptation" mean in climate context?
B) Adjusting to actual or expected climate impacts
16. Why does the text emphasize that climate impacts are "interconnected"?
A) To confuse readers with complex information
B) To show that solving one aspect (e.g., emissions) requires addressing multiple related issues
C) To argue that climate change is unsolvable
D) To focus only on economic impacts
17. How does the text's structure (causes → effects → solutions) strengthen its message?
A) It makes the text longer and more impressive
B) It logically guides readers from understanding the problem to taking action
C) It hides the most important information at the end
D) It focuses only on negative outcomes
18. What lesson about global citizenship can readers apply from this text?
A) Climate change is someone else's problem
C) Collective, multi-level action is essential to address global challenges
D) Only governments can make a difference
19. Which quote BEST shows the urgency of climate action?
A) "Climate change refers to long-term alterations..."
B) "Addressing climate change requires urgent and coordinated action at all levels."
C) "The ocean soaks up most of the heat from global warming."
D) "Deforestation, together with agriculture... is responsible for roughly a quarter of global greenhouse gas emissions."
20. Why does the text mention that "13 million people" die yearly from environmental factors?
A) To scare readers without evidence
B) To provide a concrete statistic that underscores the human cost of inaction
C) To blame specific countries for pollution
D) To suggest that health issues are unrelated to climate
"Long-term alterations in the average weather patterns"
"Deforestation reduces the number of trees that absorb CO₂"
"Increased emission of greenhouse gases from fossil fuel combustion"
Burning waste, especially at high temperatures
"Incineration of plastic and other non-biodegradable materials releases CO₂"
Growth of cities and urban areas
"With the increase in urbanization, energy demand... intensify greenhouse gas emissions"
"Some of us are already more vulnerable to climate impacts"
"One million species are at risk of becoming extinct"
"The number of people displaced by weather-related events is expected to rise"
Actions to reduce severity/impact of climate change
"Provide financial and technological support... for adaptation and mitigation"
Adjusting to actual or expected climate impacts
Gases that trap heat in atmosphere (CO₂, methane, etc.)
"Increased emission of greenhouse gases from fossil fuel combustion"
Coal, oil, natural gas formed from ancient organic matter
"Burning of fossil fuels: Coal, oil, and natural gas release large amounts of carbon dioxide"
Climate change = long-term alterations in Earth's weather patterns
Human activities are primary recent drivers
Establishes responsibility and agency for solutions
Methane is 25x more potent than CO₂ at trapping heat
Highlights importance of targeting multiple gases
~12 million hectares of forest destroyed yearly
Concrete statistic showing scale of deforestation
Transport = ~25% of global energy-related CO₂ emissions
1 million species at risk of extinction in coming decades
Shows biodiversity crisis linked to climate
Climate change = "single biggest health threat facing humanity"
Connects environmental and public health issues
Limiting warming to 1.5°C helps avoid worst impacts
Key target from Paris Agreement/UN science
Human Activities (fossil fuels, deforestation, industry, etc.)
Effects: Severe storms, droughts, rising oceans, species loss, food scarcity, health risks
Impacts on Vulnerable Populations & Global Stability
Solutions Needed: Individual + Community + Global Action
Always support answers with specific phrases or data from the text.
For cause-effect questions, trace the logical chain using text evidence.
When analyzing solutions, reference all three levels: individual, community, global.
Use statistics (12M hectares, 25x methane potency, 13M deaths) to strengthen responses.
Remember: The text emphasizes interconnectedness—link ideas across sections.
